questions.question: Explain the difference between overloaded global new/delete and class-specific new/delete with an example.
keyPoints: Global new/delete applies to all allocations in program
Class-specific new/delete only applies to that class
Class versions take precedence over global
Example shows class declaration of static operator new and operator delete

questions.question: What is Placement New? Write its syntax and explain with an example when it is useful.
keyPoints: Placement new constructs object at pre-allocated memory location
Syntax: new (address) Type(args)
Useful for: 1) Memory pools, 2) Hardware-mapped memory, 3) Reusing deleted memory

questions.question: Write the signature for overloaded new and delete operators and explain each parameter.
keyPoints: void* operator new(size_t size) - returns pointer, size indicates bytes
void operator delete(void* ptr) - ptr is memory to free
Additional forms: new[] and delete[] for arrays
size_t parameter is required for new

questions.question: What is a memory leak? How can it be prevented in C++? Explain with example.
keyPoints: Memory leak: allocated memory not freed before losing reference
Prevention: 1) Use smart pointers (unique_ptr, shared_ptr), 2) RAII principle, 3) Always pair new with delete
Example showing proper cleanup or smart pointer usage

questions.question: Explain RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) principle and its relation to memory management.
keyPoints: RAII: acquire resources in constructor, release in destructor
Memory: allocate in constructor, deallocate in destructor
Ensures automatic cleanup when object goes out of scope
Examples: smart pointers, file handles, lock guards

questions.question: What happens when you call delete on memory allocated with new[]? Explain undefined behavior.
keyPoints: Using delete instead of delete[] for arrays causes undefined behavior
Only first element's destructor may be called
Memory deallocation may be incorrect
Compiler may optimize unexpectedly leading to crashes

questions.question: Explain memory fragmentation. How does memory pooling help solve this problem?
keyPoints: Fragmentation: free memory split into small non-contiguous blocks
Prevents satisfying large allocation requests despite enough total free memory
Memory pool: pre-allocate large block, serve small allocations from it
Reduces fragmentation, improves allocation speed

questions.question: Write a complete C++ program demonstrating class-specific overloading of new and delete operators. Include proper error handling and demonstrate usage with objects of that class.
keyPoints: Define static operator new and operator delete in class
Show proper memory allocation and deallocation
Demonstrate with multiple objects
Show that class-specific version is called (not global)
Include size tracking/diagnostic output

questions.question: Explain the complete process of memory allocation and deallocation in C++ from declaration to deletion. Include: operator new, constructor, destructor, operator delete. Also explain what happens when an exception is thrown during construction.
keyPoints: Step-by-step: new allocates memory, calls constructor, returns pointer
Delete: calls destructor, calls operator delete
Exception during construction: previously allocated memory freed automatically
If constructor throws, operator new memory is deallocated

questions.question: Design and implement a simple memory pool allocator for a class. Explain the advantages of using memory pools for frequently created/destroyed objects.
keyPoints: Pre-allocate a large block of memory
Maintain free list or index for available slots
Override class-specific new/delete to use pool
Advantages: faster allocation, reduced fragmentation, better cache locality
Implementation shows pre-allocation and reuse strategy

questions.question: Compare and contrast: malloc/free vs new/delete. Also explain why mixing C and C++ memory management is problematic.
keyPoints: malloc/free: C functions, raw memory, no constructor/destructor
new/delete: C++ operators, call constructors/destructors, type-safe
Mixing: new with free or malloc with delete causes undefined behavior
C++ objects need proper construction/destruction lifecycle
Memory may appear to work but leads to crashes or memory corruption
